high_value_elements: Correctly distinguishes acute pain scales (used in-hospital by trained staff, during or shortly after the painful event) from chronic CMIs (owner-completed, measuring function and behavior over days to weeks at home)

Feline acute scales named and described: (1) Feline Grimace Scale — 5 action units (ear positioning, orbital tightening, muzzle tension, whisker position, head position), maximum score 10, rescue analgesia threshold ≥4; (2) University of Florida Short Form (UF EPS) — 10 characteristics, 3 subscales (psychomotor alteration, protection of painful area, physiological variables), maximum score 12, threshold ≤4; (3) Glasgow Composite Skin Pain Scale for cats — 28 descriptor options, 7 behavioral categories, maximum score 20, threshold ≥5

Canine acute scale named and described: Glasgow Composite Measure Pain Scale Short Form (GCMPS-SF) — 6 items, maximum score 24 (mobile) or 20 (immobile), rescue analgesia threshold ≥6 (mobile) or ≥5 (immobile)
Feline chronic CMIs named: Feline Musculoskeletal Pain Screening Checklist (6 yes/no items); Feline Musculoskeletal Pain Index (FMPI); Montreal Instrument for Cat Arthritis Testing (MICAT)

Canine chronic CMIs named: Canine Brief Pain Inventory (CBPI) — 11 items, 3 domains (pain severity, pain interference with function, quality of life); Liverpool Osteoarthritis in Dogs (LOAD) — 13 items, 2 mobility subscales; Helsinki Chronic Pain Index (11 items); COAST — Canine Osteoarthritis Staging Tool

Correctly describes FAS: Fear (F), Anxiety (A), Stress (S); elevated FAS score means pain scale scores are unreliable and may be inflated by the fearful state; intervention (anxiolytic medication, environmental modification, return visit after pre-medication) required before re-scoring
major_concerns: Cannot name a single validated pain scale for either dogs or cats
Confuses acute pain scale with CMI or does not understand the distinction in purpose or user
Cannot describe what FAS stands for or what it measures, or does not understand that high FAS inflates pain scores
Cannot name any rescue analgesia threshold score for any scale

high_value_elements: Asks specific behavioral history questions targeting chronic feline musculoskeletal pain: Can the cat jump to its usual height? Has it stopped using elevated resting spots? Has it changed where it accesses the litter box or started having accidents? Has grooming of hard-to-reach areas changed? Has interaction with family members decreased?
Applies Feline Grimace Scale during the physical examination, not before (FGS is a peri-examination tool validated in the hospital setting)

Correctly interprets a Feline Grimace Scale score of 6/10 as above the rescue analgesia threshold of ≥4; recognizes owner verbal scale of 2/10 reflects owner perception of activity level over time (chronic CMI domain) rather than in-clinic acute pain state

Differentiates chronic pain from CDS, hyperthyroidism, and normal aging using specific clinical criteria: normal thyroid panel rules out hyperthyroid; CDS presents with night vocalization and disorientation rather than musculoskeletal behavioral changes; OA pain responds partially to analgesia trial while aging alone does not
Establishes a documented baseline pain score and functional assessment allowing future comparisons to monitor treatment response
major_concerns: Relies solely on owner description without applying any validated instrument
Cannot describe behavioral history questions with highest sensitivity for feline musculoskeletal pain
Interprets owner verbal score of 2/10 as overriding the FGS score of 6/10 without understanding what each measure captures
Attributes all behavioral changes in the geriatric cat to normal aging without structured assessment

high_value_elements: Identifies prey species (rabbit, bird, rodent) pain under-recognition as the highest-priority systematic assessment failure: evolutionary hardwiring suppresses pain display to avoid predator detection; absence of vocalization or struggle does not indicate absence of pain

Applies a minimum post-procedural assessment standard to all prey species regardless of behavioral appearance: assess at fixed time intervals using available physiological indicators (heart rate, respiratory rate, posture, response to gentle palpation)
Recognizes risk of over-attributing quiet behavior to adequate analgesia in stoic breeds post-orthopedic surgery: uses physiological adjuncts (HR, RR, BP) alongside behavioral scales
Correctly interprets discordant physiological indicators (HR 140, non-weight-bearing, anorexia) as evidence of under-treated pain despite a low behavioral pain scale score
major_concerns: Cannot identify the under-recognition risk for any prey species
Interprets a quiet, non-vocalizing post-surgical rabbit or bird as necessarily pain-free
Has no strategy for assessing pain in a patient where behavioral scoring is unreliable
Cannot identify any scenario where pain could be over-attributed

high_value_elements: Explains the evolutionary basis for pain masking in prey species: mid-level predators that display weakness are targeted by larger predators; therefore behavioral suppression of pain cues is adaptive — and exactly what makes clinical under-recognition so common

Explains why owner-reported scores and clinic-based scores differ: owners observe habitual behavior over weeks (functional/activity domain); clinicians observe the immediate behavioral and facial response to examination (acute pain domain). Both are valid but measure different constructs

Justifies Feline Grimace Scale for caged or hospitalized cats: facial action units are observable without requiring the cat to locomote; removes the performance bias of locomotor-based scales in a hospital cage setting

Explains why discordance between behavioral score and physiological indicators mandates multimodal assessment: stoic breeds (e.g., northern working breeds) have been selectively bred to suppress behavioral pain expression; physiological responses are harder to consciously suppress
major_concerns: Cannot explain why prey species mask pain from an evolutionary standpoint
Cannot justify why a validated instrument is preferable to clinical intuition
Cannot explain why a discrepant owner and clinic score warrants further investigation rather than dismissal of one score

high_value_elements: Acknowledges that validated acute pain scales do not exist for all species in this fellowship (most exotic species lack validated tools; best-available adaptations must be used with explicit uncertainty)
Recognizes no single pain scale captures all pain dimensions and that combining owner-reported CMI with in-clinic acute scale provides a more complete picture
Acknowledges the challenge of distinguishing chronic pain from CDS in geriatric cats and describes how a multimodal workup and analgesia trial inform the differential
Identifies when specialist consultation is appropriate: complex neuropathic presentations, failure to respond to multimodal analgesia, exotic species requiring species-specific expertise
major_concerns: Claims to accurately assess pain in all species using the same scale developed for dogs or cats
Has no acknowledgment of the limits of any assessment tool
Cannot identify any patient type for which current validated tools are insufficient or absent

high_value_elements: Integrates WASVA pain guidelines as a clinical practice standard: pain is the fourth vital sign; assessment baseline before surgery, then every 30 minutes for 6-8 hours post-op; avoid disturbing cats that are eating or sleeping as signs of adequate pain control
Connects Epstein fellowship content on OA, neuropathic pain, and surgical pain categorization (high vs. low nociceptive burden) to pre-emptive assessment planning and analgesic protocol selection

Discusses gabapentin's effect on neurological examination: gabapentin used pre-visit for anxiolysis in cats can affect gait and postural reactions; neurological assessments must be timed to account for gabapentin half-life (4 hours in normal cats, longer in CKD)

Connects assessment accuracy directly to treatment outcome: unmeasured pain is effectively untreated pain; systematic assessment is the mechanism by which oligoanalgesia (under-treatment) is detected and corrected
major_concerns: Cannot integrate WASVA guideline recommendations into a clinical workflow
Treats pain assessment as an optional clinical extra rather than a core standard of care
Cannot describe any practice-level implementation strategy for systematic pain assessment
